title: Lesson 1: Roots of the Arab-Israeli Conflict to 1945

events.text: The rise of modern Zionism in Europe under Theodor Herzl in the late 19th century.
The outbreak of the First World War in 1914, leading to the collapse of the Ottoman Empire.
The British government issues the Balfour Declaration (1917), pledging support for a Jewish national home in Palestine.
The League of Nations grants Britain the Mandate for Palestine (1920).
Increasing Jewish immigration during the 1920s and 1930s causes friction, culminating in the 1936-39 Arab Revolt.
The British issue the 1939 White Paper, heavily restricting Jewish immigration to appease Arab allies.
The Holocaust during WWII creates immense international sympathy and pressure for a Jewish refuge.

title: Lesson 2: End of the British Mandate, 1945ÔÇô1948

events.text: The end of WWII leaves hundreds of thousands of Jewish Holocaust survivors in Displaced Persons camps (1945).
Jewish paramilitary groups like the Irgun launch an insurgency against British rule, bombing the King David Hotel (1946).
An exhausted British government hands the Palestine problem over to the newly formed United Nations (1947).
The UN passes Resolution 181, proposing the partition of Palestine into separate Jewish and Arab states.
The Jewish Agency accepts the partition plan, while the Arab Higher Committee rejects it.
Civil war breaks out between Jewish and Arab militias in Palestine (late 1947 to early 1948).
Britain officially terminates the Mandate and withdraws its troops on 14 May 1948.
David Ben-Gurion proclaims the establishment of the State of Israel (14 May 1948).
Surrounding Arab nations (Egypt, Syria, Jordan, Iraq) invade Israel immediately after its declaration of independence.
The initial Arab offensive is uncoordinated, but they manage to secure the West Bank, Gaza, and parts of Jerusalem.
The UN brokers a crucial four-week truce in June 1948, allowing Israel to organize and rearm via Czechoslovakia.
The newly unified Israel Defence Forces (IDF) launch a devastating counter-offensive when fighting resumes.
By late 1948, Israel controls 78% of Mandatory Palestine, far more than the UN Partition Plan awarded.
Over 700,000 Palestinian Arabs flee or are expelled from their homes (The Nakba).
Armistice agreements are signed in 1949, ending the fighting but without formal peace treaties.
Gamal Abdel Nasser comes to power in Egypt, promoting Pan-Arabism and defying the West.
Egypt signs the Czech Arms Deal (1955), securing massive Soviet-bloc weaponry.
The USA and Britain withdraw funding for Nasser's Aswan High Dam project in retaliation.
Nasser nationalizes the Suez Canal Company (July 1956) to fund the dam.
Britain, France, and Israel sign the secret Protocol of S├¿vres to overthrow Nasser and regain the canal.
Israel invades the Sinai Peninsula, giving Britain and France a pretext to intervene as 'peacekeepers'.
Global outrage and intense US financial pressure force Britain, France, and Israel to humiliatingly withdraw.

title: Lesson 5: The Build-up to the Six Day War

events.text: The Palestine Liberation Organisation (PLO) is founded in 1964 with the goal of destroying Israel.
Fatah, a militant wing led by Yasser Arafat, begins launching guerrilla attacks against Israel from Syria and Jordan.
Israel retaliates strongly, such as the massive raid on the Jordanian village of Samu in 1966.
The Soviet Union feeds Egypt false intelligence that Israel is massing troops on the Syrian border (May 1967).
Nasser expels UN peacekeepers from Sinai and moves Egyptian troops to the Israeli border.
Nasser blockades the Straits of Tiran to Israeli shipping, an act of war according to Israel.
Fearing an imminent multi-front invasion, Israel forms a national unity government and prepares for war.
Following the overwhelming victory in the Six Day War, Israel occupies the Sinai, Golan Heights, West Bank, and Gaza.
The UN passes Resolution 242, calling for 'land for peace', but the Arab League rejects it with the 'Three Noes'.
Nasser launches the War of Attrition (1969-1970) to grind down Israeli forces stationed along the Suez Canal.
Anwar Sadat succeeds Nasser in 1970 and decides that a limited war is necessary to force Israel to negotiate.
Sadat expels thousands of Soviet military advisors from Egypt to signal his independence to the USA.
Egypt and Syria launch a massive surprise attack on the Jewish holiday of Yom Kippur (October 1973).
Despite heavy early losses, the IDF repels the attack, prompting superpowers to intervene to stop the fighting.
The Yom Kippur War ends in a stalemate, shattering Israel's aura of invincibility and restoring Arab pride.
The 1973 Oil Embargo causes a global economic crisis, forcing the US to urgently seek peace in the Middle East.
US Secretary of State Henry Kissinger conducts 'shuttle diplomacy', brokering disengagement agreements.
Egyptian President Sadat makes a historic, unprecedented visit to Jerusalem in 1977 to address the Israeli Knesset.
US President Jimmy Carter invites Sadat and Israeli Prime Minister Begin to Camp David (1978).
After thirteen days of intense negotiations, the Camp David Accords are agreed upon.
The historic Egypt-Israel Peace Treaty is signed in 1979, the first between Israel and an Arab state.
The PLO is recognized by the UN as the sole legitimate representative of the Palestinian people (1974).
Following expulsion from Jordan (Black September), the PLO relocates its headquarters to southern Lebanon.
Israel invades Lebanon in 1982 to destroy the PLO's 'state-within-a-state' and end rocket attacks.
The PLO leadership is forced into exile in Tunis, severely weakening their direct military threat.
In 1987, a spontaneous grassroots uprising known as the First Intifada erupts in Gaza and the West Bank.
Images of Palestinian youths throwing stones at Israeli tanks damage Israel's international reputation.
The Intifada forces the PLO to publicly recognize Israel's right to exist in 1988, opening the door for diplomacy.
The end of the Cold War and the 1991 Gulf War fundamentally change the geopolitical landscape of the Middle East.
The USA hosts the Madrid Conference in 1991, bringing Israeli and Arab delegations together for the first time.
Secret back-channel negotiations begin between Israel and the PLO in Norway (1993).
The historic Oslo I Accord is signed on the White House lawn, marked by the Rabin-Arafat handshake (1993).
The Palestinian Authority is created, granting limited self-rule in Gaza and Jericho.
The Israel-Jordan Peace Treaty is signed in 1994, normalizing relations.
Tragically, Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin is assassinated by a Jewish extremist in 1995, derailing the peace process.
May 14, 1948: The British mandate ends, and Israel declares independence.
May 15, 1948: Armies from Egypt, Syria, Jordan, and Iraq invade the new state.
June 1948: The UN brokers a four-week ceasefire.
July 1948: Israel utilizes the ceasefire to reorganize the IDF and smuggle in Czechoslovakian arms.
Late 1948: The reinforced IDF launches successful counter-offensives, routing Egyptian and Syrian forces.
Early 1949: Armistice agreements are signed. Israel gains 78% of Mandatory Palestine.
Result: The Palestinian Nakba occurs, with over 700,000 refugees fleeing to neighboring Arab states.
July 1956: Nasser nationalizes the Suez Canal Company, infuriating Britain and France.
October 1956: Britain, France, and Israel finalize the secret Protocol of S├¿vres.
October 29, 1956: Israeli paratroopers drop into the Sinai Peninsula and advance rapidly toward the canal.
October 30, 1956: Britain and France issue a pre-arranged ultimatum demanding both sides withdraw 10 miles from the canal.
October 31, 1956: When Nasser refuses, Anglo-French forces bomb Egyptian airfields.
November 1956: The UN, backed strongly by the USA and USSR, demands an immediate ceasefire.
Result: A humiliating political defeat for Britain and France, while Israel secures a UN buffer force in Sinai.

title: Deep Dive: The 1967 Six Day War

events.text: May 1967: Acting on false Soviet intelligence, Nasser expels UN peacekeepers and remilitarizes the Sinai.
Late May 1967: Nasser closes the Straits of Tiran, cutting off Israel's southern port of Eilat.
June 5, 1967: Israel launches Operation Focus, a devastating preemptive airstrike that destroys the Egyptian air force on the ground.
June 6-7, 1967: Israeli armored columns sweep across the Sinai Peninsula, reaching the Suez Canal.
June 7, 1967: Following Jordanian shelling, the IDF captures East Jerusalem and the West Bank.
June 9-10, 1967: In fierce fighting, the IDF scales and captures the heavily fortified Golan Heights from Syria.
Result: Israel quadruples its territory in just six days, radically changing the strategic map of the Middle East.

title: Deep Dive: The 1973 Yom Kippur War

events.text: October 6, 1973: Egypt and Syria launch a coordinated surprise attack on the holiest day in the Jewish calendar.
October 6-7, 1973: Egyptian forces successfully cross the Suez Canal using water cannons to breach the Bar Lev Line.
October 7-9, 1973: Syrian tanks overwhelm Israeli defenses on the Golan Heights, causing panic in Israel.
Mid-October 1973: Both the USA and USSR launch massive emergency airlifts to resupply their respective allies.
October 16, 1973: Israeli forces under General Ariel Sharon cross the Suez Canal, encircling the Egyptian Third Army.
October 24, 1973: A UN ceasefire halts the fighting as Israeli forces advance toward Cairo and Damascus.
Result: A military victory for Israel but a psychological triumph for the Arabs, opening the door for future peace talks.

title: Deep Dive: The 1982 Invasion of Lebanon

events.text: Pre-1982: The PLO operates a 'state-within-a-state' in southern Lebanon, launching rocket attacks into northern Israel.
June 1982: After an assassination attempt on the Israeli ambassador in London, Israel invades Lebanon.
June-July 1982: The IDF advances far beyond their initial 40km goal, pushing all the way to the capital, Beirut.
August 1982: Israel lays siege to Beirut, subjecting the city to heavy bombardment to flush out the PLO.
Late August 1982: A US-brokered deal allows Yasser Arafat and the PLO to be evacuated to Tunisia.
September 1982: The Sabra and Shatila massacre occurs, sparking massive anti-war protests within Israel.
Result: The PLO is fractured and exiled, but Israel becomes bogged down in a costly 18-year occupation of southern Lebanon.
